name: Javanese
description: Common words, polite (krama) vs casual (ngoko) speech, and frequent slang from Central & East Java.

entries.contextual: A polite, respectful 'yes' (krama). Used when speaking to elders or people of higher status.
entries.culturalTip: Pair it with a slight bow of the head and a soft tone. Saying just 'iya' (casual) to an elder can seem rude.

entries.contextual: Casual 'yes' (ngoko). Fine among friends and people of the same age.
entries.culturalTip: Avoid using this with older people or officials — switch to 'inggih' to show respect.

entries.contextual: Polite thank you. The standard courteous expression of gratitude.
entries.culturalTip: Respond with a gentle smile and a small nod. You can reply 'sami-sami' (you're welcome).

entries.contextual: An invitation — 'please, after you' or 'help yourself'. Very common when offering food or passing someone.
entries.culturalTip: Often said with an open palm gesture pointing the way. Returning 'monggo' back is polite when passing each other.

entries.contextual: Excuse me / sorry to bother. Used before interrupting or passing in front of someone.
entries.culturalTip: When passing in front of seated elders, slightly lower your body and extend your right hand downward as you say it.
A warm casual greeting. Reply 'apik' (good) with a smile. Keep it light and friendly.

entries.contextual: A very respectful 'yes, I will do it' to a superior or elder.
entries.culturalTip: Reserved for high-respect situations. Using it casually can sound sarcastic, so keep it sincere.

entries.contextual: Slang for feeling nervous or shaky, often before a big moment.
entries.culturalTip: If a friend says this, offer reassurance with a pat on the shoulder — physical comfort among friends is welcome.
When inviting an elder to eat, use 'dhahar', not 'mangan'. Offering food first is a strong sign of hospitality.
Said warmly between friends. Acknowledge with 'siap' (ready/got it) and a thumbs-up.
